mainText:
If you only remember one thing today: Space X A I launched Grok four point five, its first Opus class frontier model, built together with Cursor after the sixty billion dollar acquisition, and priced well under its rivals.

Story one. Grok four point five arrives. From your A I News by swyx, confirmed by TechCrunch and Axios. Space X A I released Grok four point five. It's the company's first model since going public, and the first co-developed with Cursor. Musk calls it an Opus class model, but faster and cheaper: two dollars per million input tokens, six dollars per million output. It targets coding, agent work, finance, legal, and office tasks, like building complex Excel models and PowerPoint decks. It's available in Grok Build, in Cursor, and from the console. But not yet in the European Union. Why it matters: frontier level capability at commodity pricing puts pressure on every lab, and the E U holdout shows regulation now shapes launch maps.

Story two. Meta debuts Muse Image. From your Rundown and your A I Collective. Meta launched Muse Image, its first image model since Alexandr Wang rebuilt the A I lab. It's rolling into the Meta A I chatbot, Instagram, and WhatsApp. It can generate images of friends or creators from their public Instagram posts. TechCrunch reports users are already pushing back over the use of their photos. Meta points to an opt out setting and invisible watermarks. Why it matters: Meta is turning your social graph into generation material. That's a fresh consent battleground, with clear comms implications.

Story three. Open A I: G P T five point six cleared, plus a buying spree. From your Axios A I plus, via Readwise. The U S government approved Open A I's broad release of G P T five point six after a careful review. That's a notable new gate for frontier launches. Open A I is also buying companies that help businesses actually deploy A I, betting that distribution matters as much as models. Why it matters: Washington now sits in the release path of frontier models. Approval itself is becoming a competitive event.

Story four. A I safety and policy. Also from your Axios A I plus, via Readwise. Anthropic's Claude uses a hidden reasoning workspace called J Space to think silently, much like human reasoning. The same piece warns that labs are loosening safety rules even as capability grows. Meanwhile, Tech Policy Press counts one hundred nine state A I laws enacted by July first, with companion chatbots the hottest area. And the F T C is taking public comment through July thirty first on state laws that force changes to A I outputs. Why it matters: the regulatory center of gravity is shifting to the states, and the F T C fight will decide who shapes what models can say.

Story five. Chip geopolitics. Nvidia's China position has effectively collapsed. From about ninety five percent market share in twenty twenty three to zero new H two hundred shipments now. Jensen Huang says he has largely conceded the market to Huawei, which is scaling its Ascend chip to seven hundred fifty thousand units this year. Meanwhile, updated U S rules extend license requirements to Chinese firms' overseas subsidiaries. Related, from your Information newsletter: A I companies are tapping a three point five trillion dollar shadow bond market to finance the buildout. Why it matters: export controls have accelerated, not slowed, China's push for self sufficiency, and the A I buildout is now leaning on exotic debt.

Story six. Media watch. From your Noosphere, Jane Ferguson argues YouTube is not coming to save you. Everyone in media is talking about A I, and almost everyone is missing the real opportunity. From your Status newsletter, via Readwise: Mathias Döpfner is bidding for Bari Weiss's outlet, A B C is firing back at Brendan Carr, and the New York Times wrestles with its race against time. Also, from your Comms Stack: Dan shares five things he changed his mind about after a six month dive into A I. Worth a read for your own practice. Why it matters: A I is redrawing both media business models and the comms playbook you use daily.

Story seven, and today's good news. The United Nations A I for Good Global Commission held its first meeting yesterday in Geneva. Over forty founding members, co-chaired by Marc Benioff and Paul Kagame, and including Anthropic's Jack Clark. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
